_meta.full_name: Mini-Markers: A Brief Version of Goldberg's Unipolar Big-Five Markers (40 trait-adjective Big Five markers)
_meta.license: Copyrighted instrument published in Saucier, G. (1994), Journal of Personality Assessment (Taylor & Francis). The author, Gerard Saucier, distributes the full instrument and scoring key free of charge on his University of Oregon faculty page for non-commercial research/educational use (the page also invites researchers to contact him for normative values). The Mini-Markers is a selected subset of Goldberg's (1992) Big-Five adjective markers, which follow the public-domain/IPIP tradition; the items themselves are single, common English trait adjectives that are not individually copyrightable. Free for non-commercial research and educational use; no commercial license is established.
_meta.attribution: Saucier, G. (1994). Mini-Markers: A brief version of Goldberg's unipolar Big-Five markers. Journal of Personality Assessment, 63(3), 506-516. Item list and scoring key (Table 3: Varimax-Rotated Factor Loadings of 40 Mini-Marker Scale Items, N = 636) retrieved verbatim from the author's University of Oregon faculty page: https://pages.uoregon.edu/gsaucier/gsau41.pdf
_meta.scoring_notes: 40 unipolar trait adjectives, 8 per Big-Five scale. Standard instruction ("How Accurately Can You Describe Yourself?") asks respondents to write a number indicating how accurately each trait describes them on a 9-point accuracy rating scale (1 = Extremely Inaccurate ... 9 = Extremely Accurate). Scoring per the author's key: negatively-loading (reverse-keyed) items are reflected (9->1, 8->2, ... 1->9) before summing; the author's preferred method reflects reverse items, sums each scale's 8 items, and divides by 8 for the scale mean. The "keyed" field gives the sign of each item's highest varimax loading on its assigned factor from Saucier's Table 3. Factor labels (Saucier): I = Extraversion, II = Agreeableness, III = Conscientiousness, IV = Emotional Stability, V = Intellect or Openness. For Factor IV (Emotional Stability), "+" keyed items (Unenvious, Relaxed) indicate emotional stability and "-" keyed items indicate neuroticism.
_meta.cautions: All 40 items and their factor/keying were extracted verbatim from Saucier's own instrument PDF (form plus Table 3), not reconstructed from memory. Notes: (1) The 9-point rating-scale anchor legend was not present as extractable text in the PDF (it appears to have been a graphic); the 1-9 accuracy format is confirmed both by the instrument's own scoring instructions, which reference reflecting values "[9-->1, 1-->9]", and by the Saucier (1994) / Goldberg marker tradition. (2) In Table 3 the item is spelled "Extroverted" while the administered self-report form prints "Extraverted"; the verbatim item as administered is "Extraverted". (3) Factor IV is labeled "Emotional Stability" by Saucier; much secondary literature labels this pole "Neuroticism" and lists Moody/Jealous/etc. as positively keyed on Neuroticism -- keying here is expressed relative to Saucier's Emotional Stability pole (sign of the published loading). (4) This is the original 1994 U.S. Mini-Markers; the "International English Mini-Markers" (Thompson, 2008) is a separate, revised 40-item instrument and is NOT reproduced here. (5) "facet" is intentionally blank: the Mini-Markers is a domain-level measure with no facet structure.

verification.summary: adversarially fact-checked by a second agent vs primary sources (deepinme wide-collection workflow, 2026-07)
